title: Clean Fuels Weekly Commentary: Type 1 Transfers Increase WoW and LCFS Credit Prices Continue to Strengthen
content:
This Weekâ€™s Highlights

(12th January 2026 - 18th January 2026)
  • A total of 898,318 credits were exchanged as Type 1 trades, an increase of 35.99% week-over-week (WoW), while all non-zero trades decreased 27.09% WoW and a total of 1,831,449 non-zero credits were traded in CA LCFS.
  • The weekly average CA LCFS credit price stood at $58.29/MT for Type 1 transfers, up by 2.68% WoW; prices of all non-zero transfers rose by 5.87%, price stood at $59.19/MT.
  • The average LCFS spot credit price rose by 0.59% WoW; on 20th January, the spot price stood at $61.00/MT, and the benchmark price stood at $64.50/MT.
  • D3 RIN prices fell by 0.82% WoW, while D4, D5 and D6 RIN prices increased by 7.41%, 6.48% and 4.63% WoW respectively.

cCarbonâ€™s Market View

The North American market showcases landfillâ€‘toâ€‘RNG projects expansion, exemplified by Waga Energyâ€™s Oregon development. Producing around 407,000 MMBtu annually, it highlights rising demand for lowâ€‘carbon fuels. The shift from landfillâ€‘gasâ€‘toâ€‘power to WAGABOXÂ® upgrading reflects a move toward higherâ€‘value decarbonization pathways. Overall, the project reinforces RNGâ€™s growing role in the regionâ€™s climate and energy strategy.

CA LCFS Weekly Price and Volume Trend

[caption id="attachment_610551" align="alignnone" width="829"] Table 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average Type 1 transfer price increased by 2.68% WoW, rising from $56.77 to $58.29, while all non-zero transfer prices moved up from $55.91 to $59.19, a 5.87% WoW gain. Type 1 trades totaled 898,318 credits, reflecting a 35.99% WoW increase and total non-zero transaction volumes fell by 27.09% WoW, reaching 1,831,449 credits for the week. [caption id="attachment_610552" align="alignnone" width="1300"] Fig 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption]

CA LCFS Daily Average Spot Price

[caption id="attachment_610553" align="alignnone" width="1300"] Fig 2. California LCFS Weekly average spot prices and benchmark (*CC.Info OTC: Numbers are an average of broker prices reported to CC.info)[/caption] Last week, the average LCFS spot credit prices rose by 0.59% WoW, reaching $59.40/MT. On 20th January, the spot price stood at $61.00/MT. The benchmark price, representing the future price of contracts expiring at the end of 2026, stood at $64.50/MT on 20th January.

LCFS Weekly Trade Frequency and Volume

[caption id="attachment_610554" align="alignnone" width="1300"] Fig 3. Trade frequency and volume, Source: California ARB[/caption] As per the data reported by CARB last week, a total of 160 trades took place in the market, including 16 high-volume transactions. The highest reported transfer price was $100.00 for three Type 2 transactions involving 14,691, 17,342 and 34,556 credits. The lowest transfer price was $44.00 for a Type 1 transaction covering 2,000 credits. The largest trade by volume was a Type 2 transaction of 100,000 credits priced at $52.85, while the smallest was a Type 2 transfer of 6 credits priced at $51.00.

CA LCFS Monthly Volumes and Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_610555" align="alignnone" width="1300"] Fig 4. Total monthly volumes and associated prices, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average Type 1 spot price stood at $57.55 in January so far, rising from $53.08 in December. As of 17th January 2026, trade volumes for the month reached 4.53M.

CA LCFS Weekly Volume Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_610556" align="alignnone" width="1300"] Fig 5. Volume Price Trend indicator for the CA LCFS market, Source: California ARB[/caption] The Volume Price Trend Indicator (VPT) helps identify the parity between supply and demand for stock. The idea behind the indicator is to multiply the market volume of LCFS credits by the percentage change in its price. If the credit price declines, the value of the indicator falls. Across 12th January 2026 â€“ 18th January 2026, total VPT rose by 8.76% WoW, with a rise in all non-zero credit prices for the last week.

Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS): RIN Weekly Price Trend

RINs are credits used for compliance and are the â€œcurrencyâ€ of the RFS program. Renewable fuel producers generate RINs, market participants trade RINs, and obligated parties obtain and then ultimately retire RINs for the compliance list. D3 RIN prices fell by 0.82% WoW, while D4, D5 and D6 RIN prices increased by 7.41%, 6.48% and 4.63% WoW respectively. D3: Cellulosic biofuel D4: Biomass-based diesel D5: Advanced biofuels (produced from any type of renewable biomass except corn starch ethanol) D6: Conventional renewable biofuel ethanol derived from corn starch, or any other qualifying renewable fuel

Upcoming DatesÂ 

Key Clean Fuels Market Developments This Week

  • Canada CFR Fuel LCA Model : Pre-publications available for comments: Environment and Climate Change Canada carry out pre-publications to present the proposed updates to the Model ahead of its next publication scheduled for Fall 2026. This allows for increased transparency and allows stakeholder to give feedback on the proposed updates. Comments are to be provided by February 14, 2026 at modeleacvcarburant-fuellcamodel@ec.gc.ca.
  • Trump Administration Touts Push to Lower Car Prices, De-emphasize EVs: The Trump administration's top auto policy officials touted federal efforts to lower car prices by eliminating vehicle emissions regulations, as affordability remains a key concern among Americans. Automakers also face steep tariffs imposed by Trump on imported vehicles and parts. Despite the EV policy changes and new tariffs, new U.S. vehicle sales rose 2.4% in 2025 to 16.2 million vehicles. In December, the U.S. Department of Transportation proposed rolling back Biden-era fuel efficiency standards that had prodded auto companies to build more EVs to comply. The EPA is also expected to finalize a rule in the coming weeks eliminating vehicle tailpipe emissions requirements. USDOT estimates its proposal would reduce average up-front vehicle costs by $930 but increase fuel consumption by as much as 100 billion gallons through 2050, and cost Americans up to another $185 billion for fuel.
  • Canada, China Slash EV, Canola Tariffs in Reset of Ties: Canada and China struck an initial trade deal that will slash tariffs on electric vehicles and canola, as both nations promised to tear down trade barriers while forging new strategic ties during Prime Minister Mark Carney's visit. Canada is to allow Chinese EV imports at a 6.1% tariff and expects China to lower the canola tariff. Canada welcomes further Chinese investment, and Trump expresses support for the deal. The first Canadian prime minister to visit China since 2017, Carney is seeking to rebuild ties with his country's second-largest trading partner following months of diplomatic efforts.
  • RFA Comments on Proposed Amendments to Canadaâ€™s Clean Fuel Regulations: In comments submitted to Environment and Climate Change Canada (ECCC), the Renewable Fuels Association expressed strong support for Canadaâ€™s Clean Fuel Regulations (CFR) and shared its view on potential amendments to the program. The Renewable Fuels Association backed Canadaâ€™s Clean Fuel Regulations and supported adding a credit multiplier to boost domestic lowâ€‘carbon fuel production without undermining the efficient operation and fungibility of the highly integrated North American liquid fuels market. It noted S. grain-based ethanol imports have contributed greatly to the success and effectiveness of the program; in 2024, the share of ethanol imports used to comply with the CFR rose to 61 percent. RFA opposed proposals for minimum domestic content rules or discounted credits for imports, saying these would create logistical issues, reduce efficiency, and hinder cleanâ€‘fuel trade.
  • Waga Energy to Deliver Renewable Natural Gas in Eugene, Oregon: Lane County, in partnership with Emerald Peopleâ€™s Utility District (EPUD), has selected Waga Energy to build, own, operate, and maintain a renewable natural gas (RNG) production unit at the Short Mountain Landfill in Eugene, Oregon. When in operation, the plant is designed to produce about 407,000 MMBtu of RNG per year. The plant will utilize the WAGABOXÂ® technology, developed and patented by Waga Energy. Once operational, it is able to process up to 2,000 standard cubic feet per minute (scfm) of landfill gas and produce about 407,000 MMBtu (120 GWh) of RNG per year, delivering gas to the local pipeline. The project is expected to reduce up to 31,000 tons of COâ‚‚ equivalent emissions annually due to the displacement of fossil fuelsâ€”equivalent to avoiding the COâ‚‚ emissions from 2.7 million gallons of diesel consumed.
